summary: Gruber awards his 'Jackass of the Week' designation to Rush Limbaugh for accusing Michael J. Fox of faking or exaggerating his Parkinson's disease symptoms in a political ad supporting stem cell research. Gruber emphasizes that Fox's commercial is honest and powerful, and that Limbaugh's attack represents an extraordinarily low cheap shot against genuine advocacy. He addresses potential criticism that the topic is too political for Daring Fireball, clarifying that his objection is to Limbaugh's cruelty, not his politics. Gruber further dissects Limbaugh's non-apology, in which he simultaneously claimed to apologize while pivoting to accuse Fox of being exploited. The post closes by noting Limbaugh fully doubled down the next day, retracting nothing.
key_points: Limbaugh accused Michael J. Fox of acting or deliberately skipping medication to exaggerate Parkinson's symptoms in a stem cell research ad
Gruber argues the issue isn't political disagreement but rather the cruelty and demeaning nature of Limbaugh's attack on a genuinely ill person's advocacy
Limbaugh's supposed apology was actually a pivot to a new attack — that Fox was allowing his illness to be 'exploited' by Democrats
Gruber points out the absurd logic that Fox's visible illness somehow disqualifies him from advocating for research into his own disease
Limbaugh ultimately retracted nothing, fully standing by his original remarks the following day
money_quotes: I'm calling him a jackass — and it's taking a lot of restraint not to call him something far worse — because his response to Fox's honest, genuine advocacy regarding an important, serious issue is one of the lowest, most demeaning cheap shots I've ever seen.
So, according to Limbaugh, Fox's condition disqualifies him from speaking on the issue.
I.e. that it's unfair to generate support for stem cell research by showing how sad this disease is.
I'm sure Michael J. Fox will feel terribly exploited if he lives to see the day that a cure for Parkinson's Disease is found.
I'm not calling Limbaugh a jackass because I disagree with his politics on this issue.
Fox is not distorting the position of the Republicans these ads are targeting, and he is not exaggerating the promise of stem cell research.
key_insight: Attacking someone's visible suffering as performative in order to discredit their advocacy is not political disagreement — it's cruelty.
